event_name: UFC Fight Night: Song vs. Figueiredo

fights.fighter_a_bio: Song Yadong throws heavy hands in bursts and hunts takedowns when opponents back up, but he gasses in the third round and eats clean counters when he gets too aggressive. He's the Vegas pick here, but that's likely because oddsmakers respect his name—not his actual path to victory.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Figueiredo is a relentless leg-kicker who sets up submissions with pressure wrestling and chains his combos to drag opponents into deep water. Our model favors him heavily (80.2%) because he controls pace and doesn't blow energy—he's fought this exact style before and picked it apart.
fights.watch_for: If Song can land a clean hook or takedown in rounds 1–2 before Figueiredo's kicks break down his lead leg, he has a puncher's chance. After that, Figueiredo's cardio advantage takes over and Song will be fighting backwards.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Zhang Mingyang lands heavy hands and kicks from distance, using his reach to control the fight's pace from the outside. He's a massive favorite here—our model gives him 92.6% to win—because Menifield hasn't shown the footwork or timing to close the gap cleanly.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Menifield hunts takedowns and works dirty boxing in the clinch, looking to smother strikers and drag them into grappling exchanges. He's got a legit grappling advantage on paper, but he'll need to eat strikes getting inside against a rangy kicker.
fights.watch_for: Whether Menifield can chain takedown attempts without eating flush leg kicks or straight hands on the way in—if Zhang keeps distance and lands clean, it's a blowout; if Menifield closes the gap and gets him down, chaos takes over.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Pavlovich is a heavyweight anvil who wins by suffocating pressure—he walks opponents down, clinches relentlessly, and batters them against the cage before unloading ground-and-pound. Vegas and our model both see him as a heavy favorite (65%) because his raw power and wrestling translate across all fight distances.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Teixeira is a pure striker who relies on footwork and timing to land clean shots from range, but he has no reliable answer when forced into close quarters. At +380 odds, he's the underdog precisely because Pavlovich's clinch game and size will smother his ability to stay at distance.
fights.watch_for: Does Teixeira keep Pavlovich at arm's length in the first two rounds, or does Pavlovich close the gap and trap him against the cage? If Pavlovich gets inside, it's over—if Teixeira stays loose and piecing him up, upset odds tighten fast.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Kai Asakura pressures relentlessly with kicks and hand combinations, forcing opponents into the fence where his wrestling becomes a problem. Vegas and our model both like him at -307 because he's proven he can control fights from start to finish.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Smotherman lands hard counters when opponents overcommit, but he's reactive rather than aggressive—he needs Asakura to come forward recklessly to create openings. His Elo is actually slightly higher, but that hasn't translated to fight results our model trusts.
fights.watch_for: If Asakura eats a clean counter in round 1 and respects Smotherman's power, he'll slow down and become vulnerable to a scramble or reversal. If he keeps the pressure steady, Smotherman will get backed up and submitted.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Salikhov uses his length to land sharp strikes from distance, then dives into clinch work to tire opponents and land knees. The model gives him a decisive edge (68.7%) because his offense keeps pressure on throughout—he's harder to time than he looks.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Matthews counters with wrestling entries and ground control; he's comfortable eating strikes to close distance and drag fights into scrambles. His Elo rating is actually slightly higher than Salikhov's, but the model still sees him as the underdog due to Salikhov's cleaner striking offense.
fights.watch_for: Can Matthews get his wrestling engaged early, or does Salikhov's jab-and-clinch chain keep him at range long enough to accumulate damage?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Perez mixes striking combinations with timely takedowns to control pace—he's comfortable dictating range and wearing opponents down over three rounds. Vegas and our model both favor him (53.6%), banking on his ability to stay composed when fights get messy.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Sumudaerji has the tools to keep this competitive but sits as a slight underdog at +114 odds. He'll need to either catch Perez early with something clean or force exchanges where his timing and reflexes override Perez's game plan.
fights.watch_for: Whether Perez can establish his takedown threat early—if Sumudaerji respects it and backs up, Perez's striking combos will pile up points; if Sumudaerji calls the bluff and trades standing, the fight opens up unpredictably.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Yi Sak Lee comes in as the model's pick despite being a slight underdog on the Vegas board. With equal Elo credentials to his opponent, he'll need to impose his game early to build momentum.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Luis Felipe Dias is Vegas's favorite here, but only by a modest margin—the market sees this as a genuine toss-up. Both fighters are unproven at this level with identical chaos indexes, meaning anything can happen.
fights.watch_for: Whether Lee can control the pace in rounds 1-2; if Dias survives early pressure and finds his rhythm in round 3, his Vegas backing suggests he's the safer longer play.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ding Meng is operating at an even skill level with no clear style advantage—our model gives him a coin-flip matchup. He'll need to execute his game plan cleanly since both fighters are equally proven at 1500 Elo.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jose Henrique matches Ding Meng in experience and unpredictability, making this a true toss-up. With identical win probability and chaos ratings, this fight hinges entirely on who implements their strategy first.
fights.watch_for: Who controls the pace early. With neither fighter showing a clear style edge and both equally prone to variance, the first fighter to impose their game plan and stay composed will likely steal the momentum.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Aoriqileng pressures forward with heavy hands and looks to close distance—he's at his best when he can dictate pace and land clean power shots. Our model gives him a dominant 92.8% edge, though Vegas respects Haddon's credentials enough to make this closer than the data suggests.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Haddon's the betting favorite at -359 despite our model only giving him 7.2% odds, indicating Vegas sees something in his ability to counter or disrupt rhythm. He'll need to stay sharp on the outside and make Aoriqileng miss early, because getting drawn into a phone booth fight plays directly into his opponent's strengths.
fights.watch_for: Whether Aoriqileng can establish his jab and cut off the cage in round one—if he does, Haddon's in for a long night; if Haddon can stay elusive and land counters, he keeps the door open for an upset.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Rei Tsuruya hunts takedowns relentlessly and controls opponents on the mat—his grappling is the foundation of everything he does. Vegas backs him as a clear favorite (-342), and our model agrees at 52.6%, respecting how hard he makes it for strikers to stay on their feet.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jesus Aguilar mixes striking with submission threats and can adjust mid-fight depending on what's working. He's the underdog here (+268), but his willingness to trade standing gives him paths to win if he avoids getting dragged down early.
fights.watch_for: Whether Aguilar can keep the fight standing and land volume on the feet before Tsuruya closes the distance and secures a clinch—if Tsuruya gets him down, it's his fight to lose.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Angela Hill pressures constantly with high-volume striking and leg kicks, wearing opponents down over three rounds rather than hunting for knockouts. She relies on footwork and activity to rack up points, but can get caught by patient counters when she's loading up combinations.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Xiong Jingnan is a heavy hands striker who sits down on her shots and walks opponents down, mixing devastating hooks and crosses with clinch work. She's most dangerous when she controls the pace early—if she can't impose her power, she can fade as the fight goes longer.
fights.watch_for: Whether Hill's non-stop lateral movement and leg kicks disrupt Xiong's rhythm enough to survive the pocket exchanges, or if Xiong plants her feet and catches Hill coming in with a clean counter shot that forces respect.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Zhu Kangjie is virtually unknown at this level, with an Elo rating that puts him dead even with his opponent. Our model gives him a razor-thin 50.1% edge, suggesting this is a true coin flip.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Ramon Taveras sits at nearly identical credentials to Zhu—same Elo, same unpredictability rating, same lack of clear tendencies. This is as close to a statistical dead heat as UFC matchmaking gets.
fights.watch_for: Whoever controls the pace in round one likely wins this. With both fighters unproven at this stage, early aggression and establishing a rhythm matters more than refined technique.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Loma Lookboonmee is a heavy-handed striker who hunts knockouts and leans on Muay Thai clinch work to grind opponents down. Vegas likes her as a slight favorite, but she's unpredictable—fights can swing on a single exchange.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jaqueline Amorim controls fights through relentless pressure, strong wrestling entries, and top-position dominance. Our model gives her 92% odds to win because she's proven against tougher competition (Elo 1548 vs 1567).
fights.watch_for: Whether Amorim can close distance and get the fight to the mat before Lookboonmee's clinch elbows and knees pile up damage in the pocket.
